1: A new oracle section opens. הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה (hadevarim ha'elleh, these words) refers to the covenant terms — the covenant is accessible as a text, a set of specific terms that can be announced.
2: שִׁמְעוּ אֶת-דִּבְרֵי הַבְּרִית (shim'u et-divrei haberit, hear the words of the covenant): the covenant (berit) has specific terms (devarim) that must be heard and proclaimed. This chapter likely connects to Josiah's covenant renewal (2 Kings 23:1-3), though Jeremiah's emphasis falls on covenant failure.
3: אָרוּר הָאִישׁ (arur ha'ish, cursed is the man): the covenant curse formula (cf. Deut 27-28). Not hearing the covenant = falling under the curse structure that was always embedded in the covenant. Covenant is not merely a relationship but a legal structure with enforceable consequences.
4: כּוּר הַבַּרְזֶל (kur habarzel, furnace of iron): Egypt as crucible — both suffering and refinement. הֱיוּ לִי לְעָם (heyu li le'am, be my people) / אֱלֹהֵיכֶם (Eloheikhem, your God): the mutual covenant formula that runs from Exodus through Jeremiah to Revelation 21:3.
5: אֶרֶץ זָבַת חָלָב וּדְבָשׁ (eretz zavat chalav udevash, land flowing with milk and honey): the promise linked to Sinai-covenant obedience. Its loss (exile) signifies its removal. אָמֵן יְהוָה (Amen YHWH) — Jeremiah's covenant affirmation.
6: YHWH commands Jeremiah to proclaim (qara) these words in the cities of Judah and streets of Jerusalem — the covenant is public proclamation, not private instruction. The nationwide command anticipates the nationwide covenant-breaking that follows.
7: הָעֵד הַעִדֹתִי (ha'ed ha'idoti): infinitive absolute construction for emphatic repeated action — I earnestly, repeatedly warned your ancestors. The frequency of YHWH's warning heightens the culpability of Israel's refusal.
8: לֹא שָׁמְעוּ (lo sham'u, they did not listen): the covenant's perpetual problem. שְׁרִירוּת לִבָּם (sherirul libam, stubbornness of their heart): a key Jeremianic phrase for the structural resistance that makes the old covenant insufficient and the new covenant (Jeremiah 31) necessary.
9: קֶשֶׁר (qesher, conspiracy/treason): not merely individual disobedience but organized collective rejection — a political-rebellion term. The men of Judah and inhabitants of Jerusalem have returned to the sins of their first ancestors.
10: שָׁבוּ עַל עֲוֺנוֹת אֲבוֹתָם (shavu al avonot avotam, returned to the iniquities of their ancestors): covenant-breaking is generational and repetitive — confirming the diagnosis of 10:23 that humans cannot direct their own way. They went after other gods; they broke the covenant.
11: הִנְנִי מֵבִיא אֲלֵיהֶם רָעָה (hineni mevi alehem ra'ah): the hinneh particle marks the immediacy of divine action. לֹא יוּכְלוּ לָצֵאת מִמֶּנָּה — from which they cannot escape. The irrevocability is underscored.
12: The cities will cry to their gods, but the gods cannot save. The tragic irony: Israel chose gods who cannot answer in the time of need, while abandoning the God who could. כְּמִסְפַּר עָרֶיךָ (kemispar areycha) — as many as your cities are your gods.
13: As many altars (mizbechot) as streets in Jerusalem, set for Baal — directly violating the Deuteronomic principle of one worship-place. The proliferation of worship sites signals the complete breakdown of covenant exclusivity.
14: אַל-תִּתְפַּלֵּל (al-titpallel, do not pray/intercede): a striking prohibition on prophetic intercession — the covenant-breaking is so complete that even Moses-style intercession is forbidden. This is repeated in 7:16 and Jeremiah 15:1 (not even Moses and Samuel could intercede). The prohibition signals a covenant boundary has been crossed.
15: יְדִידִי (yedidi, my beloved): a term of deep covenant affection — cf. Deuteronomy 33:12; Song of Songs. YHWH calls Israel 'my beloved' even while announcing judgment. מַה-לִּידִידִי בְּבֵיתִי (mah-liyedidi beveyti): what claim does my beloved have in my house when she acts wickedly? Sacrifice cannot override unfaithfulness.
16: זַיִת רַעֲנָן יְפֵה-פְרִי תֹאַר (zayit ra'anan yefeh-peri to'ar, a thriving olive tree, beautiful in fruit and appearance): Israel as YHWH's intended planting — productive and beautiful. The olive tree metaphor recurs in Psalms 52:8 and Romans 11:17-24 (Paul's wild-olive grafting). The contrast: with fire and noise, its branches will burn.
17: YHWH himself planted (neta') Israel and now pronounces judgment because of the evil done, burning incense to Baal. The planting-pruning imagery anticipates John 15 (the vine; the Father who removes fruitless branches).
18: YHWH revealed to Jeremiah the plot of the Anathoth men against him. אָז רָאִיתִי מַעֲלְלֵיהֶם (az ra'iti ma'alelehem): YHWH's revelation is the source of Jeremiah's knowledge — the prophet could not have known by ordinary means.
19: כְּכֶבֶשׂ אַלּוּף (kekeves alluf, like a gentle/tame lamb) led to slaughter (tivach) without knowing it. This image is taken up in NT for Christ: Isaiah 53:7 ('led like a lamb to slaughter'), John 1:29 ('the Lamb of God'), Acts 8:32. נַכְרִיתֶנּוּ מֵאֶרֶץ חַיִּים (nakritenu me'eretz chayyim, let us cut him off from the land of the living): the exact phrase used in Isaiah 53:8 of the Servant.
20: יְהוָה צְבָאוֹת שֹׁפֵט צֶדֶק (YHWH Tzeva'ot shofet tzedek, YHWH of armies, righteous judge): Jeremiah commits his case to the divine judge. בֹּחֵן כְּלָיוֹת וָלֵב (bocheyn kelayot va-lev, tester of kidneys and heart): in Hebrew physiology, kidneys = deep emotion/conscience; heart = thought/will — testing both means examining the complete inner person. Cf. Revelation 2:23 (Jesus: 'I search hearts and minds').
21: The specific threat: men of Anathoth threaten Jeremiah not to prophesy in YHWH's name. The hometown opposition to the prophet is a type of the broader rejection pattern — anticipating Jesus's rejection at Nazareth (Luke 4:24: 'no prophet is accepted in his hometown').
22: YHWH's response: the young men of Anathoth will die by sword; their sons and daughters by famine. The principle that violence plotted against the prophet returns on the plotters — a microcosm of the larger oracle-fulfillment dynamic throughout Jeremiah.
23: שְׁאֵרִית לֹא תִהְיֶה לָהֶם (she'erit lo tihyeh lahem, there will be no remnant for them): the reversal of remnant hope applied to Jeremiah's persecutors. The year of their punishment (pekudatam) is coming.
